title: When Workers Lose to AI
gist: The accessible (pre-paywall) text of this piece — framed around AI-driven job loss — actually only recounts the history and 2026 dismantlement of Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA), the 1962 Kennedy-era program that aided trade-displaced workers; the article cuts off mid-sentence before addressing AI-specific policy, so this card reflects only the TAA material the author lays out as backdrop.

themes.theme: Trade Adjustment Assistance's rise and fall

insights.claim: Trade Adjustment Assistance, the U.S. government's primary program for workers who lose jobs to trade, was created in 1962 under the Kennedy administration and for six decades provided cash assistance, job training, and other services to import-affected workers.
insights.speaker: Jacob Leibenluft
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical policy template (wage support plus retraining for a specific class of displaced workers) that the author appears poised to argue should be adapted for AI-driven job losses.
insights.quote: The U.S. government had created Trade Adjustment Assistance, as the program is known, in 1962 under the Kennedy administration.
insights.locator: The U.S. government had created Trade Adjustment Assistance

insights.claim: The Trump administration effectively ended Trade Adjustment Assistance in 2026 via an obscure budget document rather than a public policy debate, with Trump's stated remedy for trade-displaced workers being tariffs rather than direct worker assistance.
insights.speaker: Jacob Leibenluft
insights.so_what: Signals the current administration is dismantling, not expanding, the safety-net tools (wage support, retraining) that could otherwise be repurposed for AI-driven worker displacement — a headwind for any AI-adjustment policy proposal.
insights.quote: President Donald Trump had long made clear that his only remedy for workers threatened by trade was new tariffs.
insights.locator: But President Donald Trump had long made clear

insights.claim: The article is framed, per its own subtitle, as an argument that the U.S. government can and should act to 'soften the blow' of AI-driven automation on workers, using the rise and fall of Trade Adjustment Assistance as its historical departure point.
insights.speaker: Jacob Leibenluft
insights.so_what: Flags this as a policy-prescription piece worth revisiting once the full text is accessible — the TAA history suggests the author will propose a redesigned adjustment program rather than reliance on tariffs alone.
insights.quote: How the U.S. Government Can Soften the Blow of Automation
insights.locator: When Workers Lose to AI / How the U.S. Government Can Soften the Blow of Automation
